Вы под атакой! Ваш союзник под атакой! Добро пожаловать, Командир! Дисклеймер Privacy Правила This is a real-world, real-time, location based game. Take care whilst playing, and always be aware of hazards around you. Playing this game whilst driving, operating machinery, cycling, or whilst in command of air or water craft may be illegal and dangerous. Observe all local laws, and do not engage in unlawful trespass or other criminal activity. You are solely responsible for your decision to play this game, and any consequences that may arise from doing so. Keep it fun, stay safe, and don't neglect other life responsibilities! Counterforce is a free game that does not engage in any marketing whatsoever, and personal information will be treated with utmost respect. Policy changes will always be by prior agreement in order to continue playing, should Counterforce ever become a marketable product. A unique, securely-encrypted key derived from your device's identity is used for the sole purpose of identifying your account. Other non-personally-identifying parameters of the device, and lists of running apps/services, may be accessed to prevent cheating. Your real world location will be shared with other players. A "Privacy Zone" feature exists which allows you to obscure sensitive locations such as home and workplaces from the game. It is advisable to make use of this facility before you begin playing, by clicking the icon on the registration screen or accessing it in the Settings menu later. It bears the following symbol: All in-game activity is logged on the server for administration, debugging and development purposes. Cooperation with the UK authorities (or their nominated parties) will be made in the highly unlikely event that they should wish to access the data for the purposes of law enforcement, security or national interests. Manipulating your GPS location ("location spoofing"), multi-accounting from one device and use of emulators are STRICTLY FORBIDDEN. These are all easily detected, and permanent IP bans, with NO PRIOR WARNING, shall apply to each. DO NOT CONTINUE IF YOU ARE USING AN EMULATOR, HAVE MODIFIED YOUR DEVICE'S IMEI/MEID OR HAVE ANY APPS INSTALLED THAT WOULD FACILITATE GPS POSITION MANIPULATION OR MULTI ACCOUNTING (Such as "Fake GPS Location" and "Parallel Space"). This is taken very seriously and there will be no more warnings! Play fair or don't play at all! Usernames, avatars or other in-game communications that are offensive, pornographic, gory or discriminatory will not be tolerated and offending players will be banned. If you have a problem with other cultures, the game provides other legitimate features with which to express your disapproval; but don't be surprised when they shoot back! The gameplay mechanics, features and configurations are subject to change at any time, without notice, for development purposes. All players are invited (and encouraged) to share their opinions on these matters via the Discord group. Exploiting "faults" with the game rules, or other forms of "gamesmanship", is actively encouraged; it makes the issues easier to identify and fix! I agree to the above, that I am solely responsible for my own actions and choice to play this game, and will exercise common sense when doing so. Device Identity Problem A unique identity could not be determined for this device, because it is either a non-cellular tablet, emulator, unsupported phone, or the "Telephony"/"Read Phone State" permission was not enabled. Unfortunately, only cellular phones (or other devices that at least take a SIM card) are supported. Emulator use is strictly forbidden. Registering an account with one will result in a permanent ban not only for that account, but for all devices that are determined to have shared the same IP or geographical address too! DO NOT CONTINUE if this is the case. Press "Quit". If you are using a regular cellular phone and thus getting this message in error, please "Quit", check that all permissions required by this app are enabled in the app settings, and try again. Please e-mail the developer or report to the Discord group if this does not fix the problem. In auto mode, the SAM battery will engage threats automatically at any time, regardless of whether you're online or alive. Switch SAM battery to semi auto?

In semi auto mode, the SAM battery will only automatically engage threats when you are offline or dead. You will have control whenever you are online and alive. Switch SAM battery to manual?

Icons to cluster (0=disable): NUMBER Permissions Counterforce requires the following permissions to be enabled: GRANTED NOT GRANTED Internet Counterforce is a multiplayer online game, and therefore requires internet access. Access Network State TCP/IP is required for Counterforce to communicate with the game server, which this permission enables. Vibrate Counterforce offers optional vibration to accompany "under attack" warnings. Storage Configuration files and other game elements are cached on your device for efficiency. Counterforce must be able to read and write to storage to facilitate this. Location Counterforce is a location-based game that uses your device's GPS to place your character in the game world. The "fine location" permission is required for this to work. Phone State Your Counterforce account requires a unique identity, which is securely derived from your device's IMEI/MEID. The "read phone state" permission enables this information to be retreived. Receive Boot Completed Counterforce raises optional "under attack" notifications. This permission enables the threat checking service to automatically re-enable itself after a reboot. Press the button below to show prompts asking you to enable the permissions. If this doesn't work, you will need to enable the permissions in Android Settings \> Apps \> Counterforce \> Permissions ENABLE PERMISSIONS Under attack alerts You will receive warnings that you or your allies are under attack. ABM Silo This object is beyond your radar range. Move closer or activate a radar station to see it. Enter in KM: Engagement Speed: This target is beyond your radar range. Move closer or activate a nearby radar station to attack it. ABM Silo This interceptor can reliably destroy ICBMs and will only automatically fire at ICBMs. (ICBMs reduce the hit chance by 40%) Warhead Yield: %s EMP Radius: Radiation: %s ICBM silo Hit rate: Accuracy Bonus: Upgrade the radar accuracy bonus from %1$s to %2$s for %3$s? Radar stations give an accuracy bonus to structures that are inside the blue circle around the radar station. Sentry guns offer close-quarters protection to you and your structures. You can increase this amount by building a radar station near by. SAM batteries store interceptors that destroy incoming missiles. By default, SAM batteries offer no accuracy bonus to their interceptors. Build a radar station near by to increase the accuracy bonus of the SAM battery, which will be added to interceptors launched from this SAM battery. The SAM will not fire on anything beyond its engagement distance, shown as an orange circle on the map. Tap the engagement distance button to set a custom range. Missile batteries hold missiles you can use to attack your enemies. They can be upgraded to a maximum capacity of 126 missile slots. Ore mines generate ore players can collect. SAM batteries store interceptor missiles and engage enemy cruise missiles with them, protecting you and your assets. Build near a radar station to increase accuracy. Gun turrets fire artillery shells at ground targets, for base defence or lighter close-range assaults. Build near a radar station to increase accuracy. Авиабаза авиабаза Bombers have long ranges and carry air-launched missiles and bombs. Supersonic bombers have a shorter range and less missile capacity than bombers, but have a much higher speed. Stealth bombers have less missile capacity than normal bombers, but have a much higher chance of evading interceptors even without the aide of flares. Tap here to change page Starting Out Welcome to Counterforce! We are glad to have you in the community. Counterforce is a sandbox game designed to simulate life-like geopolitics including land, air, and naval warfare, resource extraction, trade, and consumption, politics, all using real life GPS coordinates! There are no set missions or goals; you make your own goals as you go along. There are many different “paths” of gameplay you can pursue in any combination you want. Because there are so many options of how to play, the game might seem overwhelming at first. It's important for you to remember that all of these paths are optional, you do not need to become a master of all of them in order to succeed. The different ways to play the game include: Each of these “paths” are just broad categories of how you can play the game. These are NOT classes or load outs that you must pick. Think of them like types of ingredients that you, the player, get to pick, and you can mix and match them any way you like. Each category has its own page in this guide, so take a look around, and don't forget to ask for help if you need it. Our community is friendly and loves helping newcomers learn the ropes. You can ask for help by joining our discord or by building a Comms Tower structure that you can use to send in-game chats. Wealth generation 
Structure-based combat

Alliance politics The Main Menu The main menu is the screen you will see when you join the game. It includes the map, a top UI bar, and two rows of buttons along the bottom of the screen. The bottom buttons each take you to important screens. (Which you may already know. You are here at the game guide, after all!) 

Tap on the top bar to find a number of useful features. Tap the wealth box to be taken to an overview of your income and expenses, an inventory of all resources you currently possess, a list of all your structures, and more. 

If you are currently damaged, you can tap the health box to the right of the wealth readout to be given the option to heal yourself. 

The box to the right of the health readout shows your ping on the game server. 

Finally, in the top right is a small icon. Tap it and you will be shown a drop-down menu with settings for what icons should show up on the map, as well as a rectangle-selection tool that allows you to select many entities at once. 

That about sums it up for the main menu. Be sure to explore by tapping on various buttons. Most UI features are self-explanatory. Don't be afraid to ask for help, and keep your eye out for question mark buttons throughout the UI screens. They contain helpful information about the screens they are on. How to Make Money Counterforce offers each player numerous ways to make money in-game. Money is what is used to make the majority of purchases in-game. This is important to remember: The game does have resources, but nearly all purchases in the game are made using only money. Resources have a different effect, which is discussed below and in more depth on the resources page. 

First, each player has a basic hourly income. You can see how much your hourly income is by tapping the wealth readout in the top left of the main menu. 

Second, each day, players can call in an airdrop from the build menu. (Access the build menu via the hammer and wrench button in the bottom-left of the main menu.) This airdrop will contain money the player can collect. 

Another way players can earn income is by starting an alliance and taxing players that join it. Certain types of income are taxable, including war winnings, hourly basic income, and money picked up from the ground. Selling missiles, structures, and units is not taxable. 

The alliance tax rate has a minimum and a maximum which can be seen on the alliance management screen. 

(More information on this can be found in the “Alliances” page of the guide.) 

Alliance taxes are stored in the alliance coffer, which is only accessible by alliance leaders, who can withdraw from it as they please. When an alliance loses a war, the entire value of the coffer is given to the victor. Thus, defeating alliances in war is another way to earn income. 

When you design a ship, submarine, or aircraft, you have the option to make this design publicly available to other players for purchase. If they do so, you will receive a kickback from the purchase of your design. 

Resources are, in most cases, not used to make purchases. Instead, they have practical effects. These are discussed in-depth on the resources page. The important thing here is that since resources are practically useful, players have a desire to obtain them. Thus, producing and selling resources to other players is another mode of making money. (Resource production, processing, and transportation each have their own pages in this guide.) Alliances are a key part of gameplay. You can access information about alliances from the globe-icon button on the bottom-right of the main menu. Alliances allow you to form teams with other players. Each alliance can have an unlimited number of players. Players in alliances cannot attack each other. Players can see the units and structures of all the other players in their alliance. Your allies and their structures and units will appear cyan on the map. 

Alliances have taxation. Leaders can customize the tax rate between 15% and 90%. All members of an alliance are taxed on certain forms of income, such as hourly income and picking money up off the ground. All tax revenue is deposited into the alliance coffer. Alliance leaders can withdraw from the alliance coffer as they please, as long as the alliance is not at war and they are not in combat. 

Alliances can also form relationships with each other. "Affiliation" is a peace treaty that prevents alliances from attacking each other, just like being in an alliance with someone else. Units and structures owned by affiliated alliances show up as blue on the map. 

Alliance leaders can also declare war on other alliances they are currently not affiliated with. Wars can only be won when one side surrenders or when one side is totally destroyed. When an alliance loses a war, the money in the alliance coffer is given to the victorious alliances or whichever player gets the final kill that disbands the alliance. About Alliances Structures Structures are the basic way players can start playing the game. Structures can be built from the hammer and wrench button on the main menu. Structures can be turned online and offline. When turned online, the player will be charged the hourly maintenance value for that structure, and the structure will go to "boot" mode for %s. At the end of every hour, the player will be charged for the hourly maintenance of each structure they own. Different structures have a different maintenance cost when online. Offline structures all have an hourly maintenance cost of %s. 
If a player cannot afford the hourly cost of an online structure, the structure will go offline. If the structure is already offline, it will take damage. 

Once structure hit 0 hp, they are destroyed and will disappear. Combat Combat in Counterforce revolves around missiles, interceptors, torpedoes, and artillery shells. The concept with each of these is simple: missiles, torpedoes, and artillery shells will fire at a target and when they reach it, they will explode, dealing damage to units and structures in the blast radius. Interceptors are used to shoot down missiles and artillery shells. 
What makes combat more complicated is the types of platforms these weapons can be fired from. 

 Missiles can be fired from missile site structures, ICBM silos, ships, aircraft, players, submarines, and tanks. 

Interceptors can be fired from the same platforms, minus submarines. 

Torpedoes can be fired from ships and submarines. (There are also anti-submarine missiles that can deploy torpedoes when they reach their target.) 

Artillery shells have not been added to the game yet, but will be fired from ships and artillery gun structures. 

It would be too complicated to discuss all of these platforms and give complete information on each weapon on this page alone, so each weapon and platform mentioned here has its own page on this guide.
Offenses Counterforce offers many options for offensive operations ranging from land units to air and sea units. Missile Batteries Missile Batteries are the basic offensive option for players. They can be build through the build menu. Missile sites have upgradable reload times and missile capacities. Missile sites have a maximum upgradable slot count of %d. 
Missile Sites can hold ground-launched missiles and nuclear missiles. They cannot hold ICBMs.
ICBM Silos ICBM silos are tough structures that are used to store and launch ICBMs. They are not upgradable and have a maximum of 1 slot for a missile. They have high HP and are tough to destroy. Note: Don't spend all your money on ICBM silos right away. These structures are considered dangerous, and other players will quickly work to destroy them. In addition, ICBMs themselves are very expensive. You will not be able to afford an ICBM just because you can afford an ICBM silo.
Missile Tanks Constructed in the Armory structure, tanks are non-upgradable and hold up to %1$d missiles. They can move around the map, but they will eventually run out of fuel. Each hour, the tank will scavenge %2$s fuel from its surroundings. Tanks can be refueled by moving them close to cargo containers that contain fuel. Tanks can slowly cross water, but they will take damage each minute while doing so. 

Tanks can be repaired by moving them close to machinery or steel resources. Tanks will regenerate %3$d HP per hour.
Infantry Infantry are purchased in Cities. (See the "Cities" help page for information on how to capture cities.) Infantry do not hold missiles. Instead, infantry can be used to capture enemy structures and can fight other infantry units. Infantry can move around the map, but they can only move if they have stamina remaining. Each hour, infantry will regenerate a certain amount of stamina based on the experience level of the infantry. Stamina can also be restored by moving the infantry near food resources. Infantry can be healed by moving them near medicine resources. They will regenerate %d HP per hour. See the "Infantry" help page for complete info on infantry units.
Aircraft Aircraft are obtained from Airbases. Aircraft are completely customizable by players and can be given numerous features including radar, stealth, missiles, interceptors, and more. See the "Aircraft" help page for complete info on aircraft and airbases.
Ships Ships are constructed in Shipyards. (See the "Shipyards" help page for information on how to capture shipyards.) Ships are completely customizable by players and can be given numerous features including torpedoes, sonar, radar, missiles, interceptors, aircraft, and more. Ships cannot hold ICBMs. See the "Ships" help page for complete info on ships.
Submarines Submarines are constructed in Shipyards. (See the "Shipyards" help page for information on how to capture shipyards.) Submarines are customizable like ships, but have fewer options. All submarines possess sonar. Submarines can be equipped with missiles, torpedoes, and submarine-launched ICBMs. (SLBMs.) See the "Submarines" help page for complete info on submarines.
Artillery Guns Coming soon!
Defenses As a new player to Counterforce, defenses should be your primary concern. Building missile batteries may be tempting, but you are not going to have the strength to challenge experienced players at the start. Build your defenses first, acquire allies, then worry about offenses. 
Fortunately, Counterforce offers many options for players to defend themselves. Mix and match them as you please, or devote yourself to mastering one of them! SAM Batteries SAM batteries are the basic defensive option for players. They can be build through the build menu. SAM sites have upgradable reload times and interceptor capacities. SAM sites have a maximum upgradable slot count of %d. \SAM Sites can hold ground-launched interceptors and nuclear interceptors. They cannot hold ABMs. SAM sites will automatically fire at hostile missiles and aircraft in range.
ABM Silos ABM silos are tough structures that are used to store and launch Anti-Ballistic Interceptors for defese against ICBMs and MIRV warhead. They are not upgradable and have a maximum of 1 slot for an interceptor. They have high HP and are tough to destroy. Note: Don't spend all your money on ABM silos right away. ABM interceptors are very expensive. You will not be able to afford an ABM just because you can afford an ABM silo. ABM silos set to "automatic" mode will fire at hostile ICBMs and MIRVs in range.
SAM Tanks Constructed in the Armory structure, tanks are non-upgradable and hold up to %1$d interceptors. They can move around the map, but they will eventually run out of fuel. Each hour, the tank will scavenge %2$s fuel from its surroundings. Tanks can be refueled by moving them close to cargo containers that contain fuel. Tanks can slowly cross water, but they will take damage each minute while doing so. 

Tanks can be repaired by moving them close to machinery or steel resources. Tanks will regenerate %3$d HP per hour. Stationary SAM tanks will automatically fire at any hostile aircraft or missiles in range..
Aircraft Aircraft are obtained from Airbases. Aircraft are completely customizable by players and can be given numerous features including radar, stealth, missiles, interceptors, and more. See the "Aircraft" help page for complete info on aircraft and airbases.
Ships Ships are constructed in Shipyards. (See the "Shipyards" help page for information on how to capture shipyards.) Ships are completely customizable by players and can be given numerous features including torpedoes, sonar, radar, missiles, interceptors, aircraft, and more. Ships cannot hold ICBMs. See the "Ships" help page for complete info on ships. Ships equipped with interceptors will automatically fire at hostile missiles and aircraft in range.
Sentry Guns Sentry Guns are structures that players can build as a point-defense against missiles ad artillery shells. Sentry guns have a range of %1$s, reload time of %2$s, and an accuracy of %3$s. Sentry Guns can only shoot down aircraft that are strafing or are in kamikaze mode.
Missiles Missiles are the most prolific form of combat in Counterforce. Missiles come in many varieties with stats based on real-life missiles. You can purchase missiles in missile sites, ships, submarines, tanks, ICBM silos, and even your own player via the "backpack" button on the main menu. Missile Types Missiles come in many different varieties. In the missile purchase screen, you will see on the right side icons next to each missile option. Tap on these icons for information about what those icons mean. The same icons are displayed below. Tap each one for more info. Stealth Missiles Missiles equipped with stealth function just like normal missiles, except enemy missile defences will not automatically fire interceptors to defend against them unless they are within %s from the interceptor launcher (such as a SAM site, ship, or tank.) 

Note that the enemy player can still manually fire interceptors! Nuclear Missiles Missiles equipped with nuclear warheads are the most powerful weapons in Counterforce. They are also one of the few things that require resources to build. Each nuclear missile (and any nuclear weapon) requires a certain amount of Fissile Material resource. The amount needed is displayed underneath the price on the missile purchase screen. 
Nuclear missiles deal extreme damage to anything in their excessive blast radius. Nuclear missiles can also be toggled between ground-burst and air-burst fuzes when you are firing them. 
Air-burst creates an EMP effect, disabling structures, and it also greatly expands the blast radius at the cost of dealing less damage. 
Ground-burst explosions have no EMP effect, have a smaller blast radius, but deal much greater damage. They will also create radiation wherever they detonate. 

Nuclear weapons will likely result in retaliation in-kind, so be very careful before deciding to use one! Nuclear missiles cannot be launched from players. ICBMs These are the most powerful weapons in Counterforce, and also the most expensive and difficult to obtain. ICBMs can only be launched from ICBM Silos and submarines. 

ICBMs contain Multiple Independently-targettable Re-entry Vehicles (MIRVs). These are extra warheads that the player can send to different targets from one missile. Each ICBM contains a different number of warheads. Some have just one, while others contain up to 15 warheads. 

ICBMs are unique in that they have to accelerate from 0 velocity at launch to their max speed. When they reach max speed or half of the distance to their target, they will deploy their warheads. Once ICBMs reach their top speed, they are too fast to be intercepted by anything other than ABM interceptors. (These are explained in further detail on the "Interceptors" help page.) 

IMPORTANT NOTE: ICBMs that are travelling slowly CAN BE INTERCEPTED BY NORMAL INTERCEPTORS. When firing an ICBM at a close target, they will have the opportunity to intercept your ICBM with cheap, slow interceptors. Anti-Submarine Missiles These missiles are designed to destroy submerged submarines that are immune to damage from other missiles. Anti-submarine missiles come in two varieties: nuclear-armed and torpedo-armed. 

Nuclear armed anti-submarine missiles have a bigger blast radius and explode directly on reaching their target location, dealing heavy damage to any submarines in range. 

Torpedo-armed ASMs will deploy a homing torpedo when tey reach their target. The torpedo will then function as described on the "Torpedoes" help page, searching for a target to home in on. Tracking Missiles Tracking missiles, unlike normal missiles, tracking missiles can follow targets to strike them precisely. They are capable of targetting tanks, trucks, players, and players. 

NOTE: Tracking missiles cannot target ships or submarines. They are only capable of tracking land targets. Anti-Ship Missiles Anti-ship missiles are tracking missiles that are capable of tracking ships and surfaced submarines. They can also track land targets. However, anti-ship missiles are very expensive, so they should be used on the appropriate target for highest efficiency. ECM Missiles These are missiles equipped with electronic countermeasures that reduce interceptor accuracy by %s. Interceptors Interceptors are the most common form of defense in Counterforce. Interceptors can be fired from ships, tanks, SAM sites, and ABM silos. Interceptors can also be purchased for your own player via the button on the main menu. Non-nuclear interceptors have an accuracy of %s. 
Interceptors can only fire at missiles that are SLOWER than they are. This includes ICBMs that are in the boost phase still accelerating to their top speed. As long as the ICBM is slower than a given inteceptor, that interceptor can strike the ICBM, making them very useful for short-range ICBM defense. Interceptor Types Interceptors come in ship, air, tank, and ground-launched varieties. Interceptors can be nuclear-armed and they can be classified as anti-ballistic missiles. Once ICBMs reach full speed OR deploy their warheads, ABMs are required to shoot them down. Nuclear Interceptors Nuclear-armed interceptors, like nuclear missiles and torpedoes, require fissile material resources in order to build. The amount needed is listed underneath the interceptor price in the purchase screen. Nuclear interceptors have a 100\% chance of shooting down whatever missile or aircraft they are targetted at. Additionally, they have a 50\% chance of destroying other missiles and aircraft in the blast radius. Anti-Ballistic Interceptors Interceptors classified as anti-ballistic are capable of targetting ICBMs and MIRV warheads. They come in nuclear and non-nuclear varieties. Conventional ABMs have the standard interceptor accuracy, while the nuclear variants have a 100\% kill chance against the warheads and ICBMs they are targetting. The area-effect damage that nuclear ABMs have makes them useful for destroying clusters of MIRV warheads as they are deployed.
Torpedoes Torpedoes are specialized anti-naval weapons. Torpedoes deal extreme damage to submarines and ships and come in homing, non-homing, nuclear, and non-nuclear varieties. They are available for purchase only for ships and submarines. Certain missiles can also deploy torpedoes when they reach their target, as noted in the "Anti-Submarine" section on the "Missiles" help page. Torpedoes have a certain range, and once they have travelled as far as their range allows, they will disappear. If a torpedo strikes land, it will explode. 

Warning regarding torpedoes: These are non-discriminating weapons, meaning that if you or your allies sail your ships or submarines near one, it will explode and damage or destroy your vessel. Nuclear Torpedoes Like nuclear missiles and interceptors, nuclear torpedoes require fissile material to build. Nuclear torpedoes have an excessive blast radius and can deal high damage to submarines and ships in a large area. Be careful using these, as once again they will not discriminate friend from foe when exploding. Nuclear torpedoes can damage structures if they are in the blast radius, such as if the torpedoes explodes on a coastline. Homing Torpedoes These torpedoes are capable of tracking a naval target and chasing them down before exploding. When firing a homing torpedo, select an entity or location to send the torpedo towards. When it reaches this area, it will begin circling in the water, looking for a target. It can home in on targets within %s. Torpedoes can randomly lose their target each second, so if you are the target of a torpedo, put distance between it and your ship to give yourself time to escape. 
WARNING: Homing torpedoes do not distinguish friend from foe. Your homing torpedo can and will lck on to friendly ships and submarines if they are available and in-range.
Cities Cities are located on the map in their real-life locations for players to travel to to capture. Cities produce infantry for players, and structures built within %s of a city that you or your allies occupy incur no maintenance cost. (Note: The zero-maintenance benefit is a feature that has not been implemented yet.) 

If cities are irradiated by nuclear weapons or attacked directly, they will rapidly lose population. Radiation is the quickest way to kill the population of a city.
Shipyards Shipyards are located on the map in their real-life locations for players to travel to to capture. Shipyards are used to design and build ships and submarines. In order to re-arm with weaponry of any kind, ships and submarines must be surfaced and within %1$s of a FRIENDLY shipyard. Ships and submarines near these friendly shipyards will repair at a rate of %2$d HP per hour. Capturing A Shipyard Shipyards, like cities, work like "king of the hill." To capture a shipyard, players must build structures within %s of the shipyard, as denoted by the circle around the shipyard that can be seen when selecting it. 
Once you have built a structure around the shipyard, it becomes yours. This means that you must travel to the shipyard in-person to capture it. Note however that once you build a structure near a shipyard, other players can use infantry to capture your structures, which will give them ownership of the shipyard. 
If there are many structures belonging to different players near the shipyard, the shipyard will revert to neutral status and become "contested", meaning no player gets the benefit of owning it. 
If all structures near a shipyard belong to different players but those players are in an alliance, the player with the most structures will be given ownership, and all their allies nearby will get the benefit as well. 

 Once a player owns the shipyard, they can use it to design and construct ships and submarines. Shipyards stocked with machinery will produced ships and submarines faster.
Designing Ships and Submarines When a player captures a shipyard, both the player and all their allies have access to use the shipyard to design naval vessels. (Note: Currently, shipyards can only produce one ship at a time. In the future they will be able to produce multiple different types of ships at a time.) 

The design process is fairly self-explanatory via help prompts on the design screen. When designing a vessel, tap on the various customization options to see a description of how they affect the design. 
When designing a ship, each feature you add to the ship increases its displacement. The displacement of a ship determines its price, production time, and HP. 

Once you are done with your design, you will need to pay a design fee in order to save the design for fast future use, or you can purchase a one-off model of the ship. Should you choose to save your design, you can make your design publicly available. Publicly available designs can be purchased by other players, and whenever one of these designs is purchased the designer will receive a profit from the purchase.
Ships [Dev note: It is not clear to me yet what features about ships actually need explaining. You customize them. They can have missiles/interceptors/torpedoes/aircraft/CIWS guns, etc. Not complicated. This page will stay blank until players start asking questions about them, which will give me a better idea of what to say here. -Biscuit]
Submarines Submarines are stealthy, lethal weapons that are hard to obtain. They are meant for high-skill players who are comfortable with taking a lot of risk for the potential of great reward. Submarines can be armed with missiles, torpedoes, and submarine-launched ballistic missiles. While submerged, a submarine is only vulnerable to homing torpedoes and anti-submarine missiles. Sonar pings from enemy vessels however can detect submarines. Submarines cannot fire missiles or SLBMs while submerged. Be careful when using submarines: they allow you to potentially bring devastation to entire navies and entire alliances via nuclear weapons, but they are very expensive, and it may only take one cheap torpedo to throw away all that you've invested into one. 

Nuclear-armed submarines guarantee the ability to retaliate against enemies that destroy your land and surface naval units. They may think they've won, but thanks to your hidden SLBMs, you'll get the last laugh.
Infantry Infantry units can be used to attack other infantry units or to capture enemy structures. 

Infantry units gain experience as they kill other infantry units and capture structures. The more experience the units gain, the more damage they deal in combat, the less damage they take, and the more health they regenerate at the end of each hour. 

Infantry units have stamina, which functions as their equivalent of fuel. When they run out of stamina, they stop moving until it regenerates. Infantry units regenerate a minimum of %1$s of their stamina and %2$d HP per hour. Move the unit near food resources to instantly regenerate stamina or near medicine resources to instantly regenerate HP. 

Infantry units are produced in Cities. Infantry Combat Infantry combat is moderately complex, with several factors impacting how much damage each unit deals to the other. 
You will see in the above image the multi-colored diamond around the infantry unit. These diamond shows what direction the infantry is facing, as well as its flanks and rear. Attacking from the front of the infantry means you will deal no extra damage. Attacking from the flanks deals 50% more damage. Attacking from the rear deals double the damage.
Cargo Trucks Trucks are produced in warehouse structures and are used for transporting resources, missiles, and interceptors. 

They function similarly to tanks in that each hour, they will regenerate %1$s of fuel and %2$d HP. Fuel and HP can be restored immediately by directing the truck to fuel and steel or machinery resources, respectively.
Resources Resources are used for many practical purposes in the game. It is important to note that resources are, for the most part, not used to purchase things in the game. This is intended to keep the game as simple as possible, so that players are not forced to learn a complex resource system in order to play the game. (Some things do require resources to purchase, such as nuclear weapons and resource processing structures like Oil Refineries and Foundry structures.)
Prospecting To produce resources, you must first prospect for resource deposits. You can find the prospect button in the top of the build menu. The kind of terrain you are currently positioned on determines what types of resource deposits you are likely to find. You can prospect for resources once every 7 days. 
To see what type of terrain you are standing on, tap the map near your player. "Common" resource types have a %1$s chance of spawning. Uncommon types have a %2$s. Uranium has a %3$s chance, except in the desert, where uranium has a %4$s probability. 

Plains: Common types: Crops. 
Forest: Common types: Lumber. 
Mountains: Common types: Iron, Coal, Concrete. 
Desert: Common types: Oil. Increased chance to find Uranium.
Resources Types and Uses Resources come in many varieties and are used for different purposes. 

Crops: Used to produce food and medicine. 
Iron: Used to produce steel. 
Coal: Used to produce electricity and steel. 
Oil: Used to produce machinery and fuel. 
Steel: Used in the production of machinery. Repairs trucks and tanks instantly, even in combat. 
Electricity: Boosts the boot time of structures significantly. Provides power to railguns. 
Uranium: Used to produce fissile material. 
Concrete: Used to produce construction supplies and used in the construction of several processing structures. 
Lumber: Used to produce construction supplies and used in the construction of several processing structures. 
Food: Restores infantry stamina and is used in the production of medicine. 
Fuel: Refuels trucks, tanks, ships, and aircraft. 
Construction Supplies: Used to build structures remotely via construction crews. [Not yet implemented.] 
Electronics: Used to build satellites and to enable instant money transfers in banks. [Not yet implemented.] 
Medicine: Heals players and infantry instantly, even in combat. 
Machinery: Used to boost production times of naval vessels, tanks, aircraft, and trucks. Repairs tanks and trucks instantly, even in combat. 
Fissile Material: Used to produce electricity and nuclear weapons.
Resource Extractors Once you have identified a resource deposit, you must build an extracting structure nearby to extract resources from the deposit. The structure must be within %1$s of the deposit. Each extractor built will produce %2$s of resources per hour, which must then be transported to storage or processing facilities.
Resource Processors Processors are a class of structure that takes raw material resources and converts them into a new type of resource. 

Processors come in several types: 

Granary: Processes crops into food. 
Foundry: Turns iron and coal into steel. 
Oil Refinery: Turns oil into fuel. 
Construction Yard: Turns lumber and concrete into construction supplies. 
Laboratory: Turns food into medicine. 
Machine Shop: Turns steel and oil into machinery. 
Lithography Plant: Converts electricity and machinery into electronics. 
Power Plant: Turns coal into electricity. 
Enrichment Facility: Converts uranium into fissile material. 
Nuclear Power Plant: Converts fissile material into significant amounts of electricity.
Update Schedule/Planned Features This is the alpha build of Counterforce. We have many more features to implement. The following list is a basic roadmap we plan to follow. We may at times add features to this list, develop features slightly out of order, or remove planned features altogether. This list is intended as a rough guide to give players some idea of where we are headed. 

Coming updates, roughly in order: 

Make Ships Capturable. 
Allow ships to refuel eachother. 
Allow players to transfer ownership of units to eachother. 
Adjust weapon prices. 
Artillery guns. 
Placeable map waypoints. 
[Possible] Remote Construction. 
Banks. 
Rail-yards and trains. 
Pipelines. 
Username/password login. 
Achievements. 
Unlockable taunts. 
Capturable monuments with benefits. 
History tracker. 
Launch Facilities and Satellites.
Cargo Inventory
